cases.title: The Fruity Breath
cases.presentation: A 17-year-old boy has felt increasingly unwell over the past day, with excessive thirst, frequent urination, and abdominal pain. His breathing is deep, rapid, and labored, and you notice a fruity odor on his breath.
cases.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: A: HHNS, since his glucose is very high.
B: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) — Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, abdominal pain, and 1-day onset are classic, possibly a first presentation of Type 1 diabetes.
D: A respiratory infection unrelated to glucose.
cases.explanation: Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid, labored breathing) occur in DKA, not HHNS or hypoglycemia. Combined with the fruity (ketotic) breath odor, abdominal pain, and roughly one-day onset, this presentation is classic DKA — possibly the first sign of previously undiagnosed Type 1 diabetes in this teenager.
cases.clinicalPearl: Kussmaul breathing plus fruity breath is DKA's signature — HHNS doesn't breathe like this.

cases.title: Days of Confusion in an Elderly Patient
cases.presentation: An 82-year-old woman with no known diabetes history has become progressively more confused over three days, with poor oral intake and dry mucous membranes. Her breathing is unremarkable — not deep or labored.
cases.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: A: DKA, given the extremely high glucose.
B: HHNS — extreme hyperglycemia and profound dehydration with an insidious multi-day onset in an older adult, without Kussmaul respirations, suggests undiagnosed Type 2 diabetes.
D: A primary stroke unrelated to glucose.
cases.explanation: The absence of Kussmaul respirations, the insidious multi-day onset, profound dehydration, and extreme hyperglycemia in an elderly patient point to HHNS rather than DKA. HHNS is classically associated with undiagnosed Type 2 diabetes and can produce severe altered mental status from dehydration and hyperosmolarity alone.
cases.clinicalPearl: Extreme glucose plus days of decline plus normal breathing, in an older adult — think HHNS, not DKA.

cases.title: Sudden Weakness Reversed
cases.presentation: A 60-year-old insulin-dependent diabetic is found with left-sided facial droop and slurred speech that began 15 minutes ago per his wife. His glucose reads 32 mg/dL. After receiving glucose, his facial droop and speech fully normalize within 10 minutes.
cases.question: What does the full resolution after glucose administration tell you?
options: A: This confirms a true ischemic stroke.
B: This confirms the presentation was a hypoglycemic stroke mimic, not a true stroke, since true strokes do not resolve with glucose.
C: The glucose reading was likely inaccurate.
D: No further monitoring is needed now that he has improved.
cases.explanation: Diabetic emergencies can present with stroke-like symptoms. The rapid, complete resolution of his facial droop and slurred speech after glucose administration confirms this was a hypoglycemic mimic — a true stroke would not respond this way. Continued monitoring is still warranted given the rapid swings in glucose.
cases.clinicalPearl: If glucose fixes the 'stroke' in minutes, it wasn't a stroke — it was hypoglycemia wearing a stroke's mask.
